what: A simple example to get started
title: Get started with pipeflow
text: example, ’ll use base R’s airquality dataset. goal create analysis pipeline performs following steps: add new data column Temp.Celsius containing temperature degrees Celsius fit linear model data plot data model fit. following, ’ll show define run pipeline, inspect output specific steps, finally re-run pipeline different parameter settings, one selling points using pipeline.

title: Get started with pipeflow
text: easier understanding, go step step. First, create new pipeline name “-pipeline” add data step provides input dataset. step add, minimum specify name step function defines computed step. Let’s take first look pipeline. , step represented one row table denoted first column. depends column lists dependencies step, empty data step since depend step (dependencies later). column eventually contain output step, currently NULL since haven’t run pipeline yet, state column shows current, initially new steps. Next, add step called data_prep, consists function takes output data step first argument, adds new column returns modified data output. refer output earlier pipeline step, just write name step preceded tilde (~) operator, , ~data case. Since pip_add works “reference”, can add step follows: , second step called data_prep added depends data step now visible column depends. Next, want add step called model_fit fits linear model data. function takes output data_prep defines parameter xVar, used specify variable used predictor linear model. Lastly, add step called model_plot, plots data linear model fit. function uses output model_fit data_prep step. also defines xVar parameter parameter title, used title plot. last line, see model_plot step depends model_fit data_prep step. addition tabular output, {pipeflow} also provides graphical representation compatible visNetwork package. particular, pip_get_graph() function returns list arguments can feed directly visNetwork::visNetwork(). , pipeline visualized directed acyclic graph (DAG) nodes represent steps edges represent dependencies.
code: library(pipeflow)  pip <- pip_new("my-pip")  pip <- pip_add(pip,     step = "data",     fun = function(data = airquality) data ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (1 step) # ------------------------------ #    step depends    out state # 1: data         [NULL]   new pip |> pip_add(     "data_prep",     function(x = ~data) {         replace(x, "Temp.Celsius", (x[, "Temp"] - 32) * 5 / 9)     } ) pip |> pip_add(     "model_fit",     function(         data = ~data_prep,         xVar = "Temp.Celsius"     ) {         lm(paste("Ozone ~", xVar), data = data)     } ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (3 steps) # ------------------------------- #         step   depends    out state # 1:      data           [NULL]   new # 2: data_prep      data [NULL]   new # 3: model_fit data_prep [NULL]   new pip |> pip_add(     "model_plot",     function(         model = ~model_fit,         data = ~data_prep,         xVar = "Temp.Celsius",         xLab = "Temperature in degrees Celsius",         title = "Linear model fit"     ) {         require(ggplot2, quietly = TRUE)         coeffs <- coefficients(model)         ggplot(data) +             geom_point(aes(.data[[xVar]], .data[["Ozone"]])) +             geom_abline(intercept = coeffs[1], slope = coeffs[2]) +             labs(title = title, x = xLab)     } ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (4 steps) # ------------------------------- #          step             depends    out state # 1:       data                     [NULL]   new # 2:  data_prep                data [NULL]   new # 3:  model_fit           data_prep [NULL]   new # 4: model_plot model_fit,data_prep [NULL]   new library(visNetwork) do.call(visNetwork, args = pip_get_graph(pip)) |>     visHierarchicalLayout(direction = "LR")

title: Get started with pipeflow
text: Even moderately complex analysis consisting , say, 15 20 different functions, keeping track different analysis parameters can quickly get hand. see, {pipeflow} becomes much easier, since pipeline keeps track parameters values. Let’s first inspect parameters defined pipeline using pip_get_params() function. returns list independent parameters (data, xVar, title). independent mean parameters don’t depend steps (.e. steps defined ~ operator). important never want mess parameters defined terms steps. Furthermore, parameter listed , even used multiple steps1. change independent parameter, simply call pip_set_params(): {pipeflow} automatically propagates parameter change steps use respective parameter. addition, recognize steps affected parameter change mark outdated. can see model_fit model_plot steps now state outdated (graphically indicated orange color). update results, just run pipeline . outdated steps re-run expected output updated accordingly now showing new x-variable Solar.R.  closer look run log shows pipeline skipped first two steps ran steps outdated, basically can thought caching mimicking behavior make software development. , {pipeflow} always keeps track steps outdated re-runs steps downstream dependencies, can huge time saver larger pipelines2. Let’s visit examples parameter changes effects pipeline. just change title plot, model_plot step needs rerun.  change input data parameter data step, since steps depend , expect steps rerun.  Last least let’s try set parameters don’t exist pipeline, mostly happens due accidental misspells. see, warning given user hinting respective parameter names, makes fixing misspells straight-forward. Next, let’s see modify pipeline.

title: Modifying existing pipelines
text: Let’s say want insert new step data_prep step standardizes y-variable. standardize step now part pipeline, far used step.
code: pip |> pip_add(     "standardize",     function(         data = ~data_prep,         yVar = "Ozone"     ) {         data[, yVar] <- scale(data[, yVar])         data     },     after = "data_prep" ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (5 steps) # ------------------------------- #           step             depends                out state # 1:        data                     <data.frame[10x6]>  done # 2:   data_prep                data <data.frame[10x7]>  done # 3: standardize           data_prep             [NULL]   new # 4:   model_fit           data_prep           <lm[13]>  done # 5:  model_plot model_fit,data_prep  <ggplot2::ggplot>  done library(visNetwork) do.call(visNetwork, args = pip_get_graph(pip)) |>     visHierarchicalLayout(direction = "LR", sortMethod = "directed")

title: Modifying existing pipelines
text: Let’s revisit function definition model_fit step use standardized data, need change data dependency refers standardize step. Also instead fixed y-variable model, let’s pass parameter. model_plot step needs updated similar way. updated pipeline now looks follows. see model_fit model_plot steps now use (.e., depend ) standardized data. Let’s re-run pipeline inspect output.
code: pip[["model_fit", "fun"]] # function (data = ~data_prep, xVar = "Solar.R")  # { #     lm(paste("Ozone ~", xVar), data = data) # } pip |> pip_replace(     "model_fit",     function(         data = ~standardize, # <- changed data reference         xVar = "Temp.Celsius",         yVar = "Ozone" # <- new y-variable     ) {         lm(paste(yVar, "~", xVar), data = data)     } ) pip |> pip_replace(     "model_plot",     function(         model = ~model_fit,         data = ~standardize, # <- changed data reference         xVar = "Temp.Celsius",         yVar = "Ozone", # <- new y-variable         title = "Linear model fit"     ) {         coeffs <- coefficients(model)         ggplot(data) +             geom_point(aes(.data[[xVar]], .data[[yVar]])) +             geom_abline(intercept = coeffs[1], slope = coeffs[2]) +             labs(title = title)     } ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (5 steps) # ------------------------------- #           step               depends                out state # 1:        data                       <data.frame[10x6]>  done # 2:   data_prep                  data <data.frame[10x7]>  done # 3: standardize             data_prep             [NULL]   new # 4:   model_fit           standardize             [NULL]   new # 5:  model_plot model_fit,standardize             [NULL]   new pip_set_params(pip, params = list(xVar = "Solar.R", yVar = "Wind")) pip_run(pip) # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.559 UTC]: Start run of pipeflow_pip 'my-pip' # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.560 UTC]: Step 1/5 data - skipping done step # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.560 UTC]: Step 2/5 data_prep - skipping done step # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.560 UTC]: Step 3/5 standardize # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.562 UTC]: Step 4/5 model_fit # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.564 UTC]: Step 5/5 model_plot # info [2026-06-13 17:22:57.574 UTC]: Finished run of pipeflow_pip 'my-pip' pip[["model_fit", "out"]] |> coefficients() #  (Intercept)      Solar.R  #  0.979672739 -0.006625601 pip[["model_plot", "out"]]

title: Modifying existing pipelines
text: Let’s see pipeline . trying remove step, {pipeflow} default checks step used step, raises error removing step violate integrity pipeline. enforce removing step together downstream dependencies, can use recursive argument. Naturally, last step never downstream dependencies, can removed without issues. Replacing steps pipeline shown vignette allow re-use existing pipelines adapt programmatically new requirements. Another way re-using pipelines combine , shown Combining pipelines vignette.
code: pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (5 steps) # ------------------------------- #           step               depends                out state # 1:        data                       <data.frame[10x6]>  done # 2:   data_prep                  data <data.frame[10x7]>  done # 3: standardize             data_prep <data.frame[10x7]>  done # 4:   model_fit           standardize           <lm[13]>  done # 5:  model_plot model_fit,standardize  <ggplot2::ggplot>  done try(pip_remove(pip, "standardize")) # Error in pip_remove(pip, "standardize") :  #   cannot remove step 'standardize' because the following steps depend on it: 'model_fit', 'model_plot' pip_remove(pip, "standardize", recursive = TRUE) # Removing step 'standardize' and its downstream dependencies: 'model_fit', 'model_plot' pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (2 steps) # ------------------------------- #         step depends                out state # 1:      data         <data.frame[10x6]>  done # 2: data_prep    data <data.frame[10x7]>  done last_step <- tail(pip[["step"]], 1) pip_remove(pip, last_step) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (1 step) # ------------------------------ #    step depends                out state # 1: data         <data.frame[10x6]>  done

title: Combining pipelines
text: Let’s define one pipeline used data preprocessing one modeling. Data preprocessing pipeline: Modelling pipeline:
code: library(pipeflow)  pip1 <- pip_new("preprocessing") |>     pip_add(         "data",         function(data = airquality) data     ) |>     pip_add(         "data_prep",         function(data = ~data) {             replace(data, "Temp.Celsius", (data[, "Temp"] - 32) * 5 / 9)         }     ) |>     pip_add(         "standardize",         function(             data = ~data_prep,             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             data[, yVar] <- scale(data[, yVar])             data         }     ) pip1 # <pipeflow_pip> preprocessing (3 steps) # -------------------------------------- #           step   depends    out state # 1:        data           [NULL]   new # 2:   data_prep      data [NULL]   new # 3: standardize data_prep [NULL]   new pip2 <- pip_new("modeling") |>     pip_add(         "data",         function(data = airquality) data     ) |>     pip_add(         "fit",         function(             data = ~data,             xVar = "Temp",             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             lm(paste(yVar, "~", xVar), data = data)         }     ) |>     pip_add(         "plot",         function(             model = ~fit,             data = ~data,             xVar = "Temp",             yVar = "Ozone",             title = "Linear model fit"         ) {             require(ggplot2, quietly = TRUE)             coeffs <- coefficients(model)             ggplot(data) +                 geom_point(aes(.data[[xVar]], .data[[yVar]])) +                 geom_abline(intercept = coeffs[1], slope = coeffs[2]) +                 labs(title = title)         }     ) pip2 # <pipeflow_pip> modeling (3 steps) # --------------------------------- #    step  depends    out state # 1: data          [NULL]   new # 2:  fit     data [NULL]   new # 3: plot fit,data [NULL]   new

title: Combining pipelines
text: Next combine two pipelines using pip_bind(). First , note data step second pipeline renamed automatically avoid name clashes. particular, first step second pipeline renamed data data2 (line 4 step column) likewise data-dependencies second pipeline updated (see lines 5-6 depends column). , binding two pipelines, {pipeflow} ensures step names remain unique resulting combined pipeline therefore automatically renames duplicated step names necessary. Now, can also seen graphical representation pipeline, two pipelines yet connected. make actual use combined pipeline, use output first pipeline input second pipeline, , want use output standardize step data parameter input data2 step. achieve , apply replace function described earlier vignette modify pipeline:
code: pip <- pip_bind(pip1, pip2)  pip # <pipeflow_pip> preprocessing-modeling (6 steps) # ----------------------------------------------- #           step       group   depends    out state # 1:        data        data           [NULL]   new # 2:   data_prep   data_prep      data [NULL]   new # 3: standardize standardize data_prep [NULL]   new # 4:       data2        data           [NULL]   new # 5:         fit         fit     data2 [NULL]   new # 6:        plot        plot fit,data2 [NULL]   new library(visNetwork) do.call(visNetwork, args = pip_get_graph(pip)) |>     visHierarchicalLayout(direction = "LR") pip |> pip_replace("data2", function(data = ~standardize) data)  pip # <pipeflow_pip> preprocessing-modeling (6 steps) # ----------------------------------------------- #           step     depends    out    state # 1:        data             [NULL]      new # 2:   data_prep        data [NULL]      new # 3: standardize   data_prep [NULL]      new # 4:       data2 standardize [NULL]      new # 5:         fit       data2 [NULL] outdated # 6:        plot   fit,data2 [NULL] outdated

title: Combining pipelines
text: Since name re-routed step might always known1, {pipeflow} package also provides relative position indexing mechanism, allows rewrite command follows: Generally speaking, relative indexing mechanism allows refer steps positioned current step. index ~-1 can interpreted “go one step back”, ~-2 “go two steps back”, .
code: pip |> pip_replace("data2", function(data = ~ -1) data)  pip # <pipeflow_pip> preprocessing-modeling (6 steps) # ----------------------------------------------- #           step     depends    out    state # 1:        data             [NULL]      new # 2:   data_prep        data [NULL]      new # 3: standardize   data_prep [NULL]      new # 4:       data2 standardize [NULL]      new # 5:         fit       data2 [NULL] outdated # 6:        plot   fit,data2 [NULL] outdated

title: Combining pipelines
text: Another way re-use steps pipelines cherry-picking, can done via pip_add_from, example: Note cherry-pick approach useful, contrast pip_bind command, renames data data2, cherry-picked fit plot steps still refer initial data step. scenarios, however, might exactly want. Generally, creating pipelines, lot steps calculating intermediate results steps contain final output interested (see e.g. plot output example). see {pipeflow} allows conveniently tag, collect possibly group final outputs, see next vignette Collecting filtering output.
code: pip <- pip_new("cherry-picked-from-1-and-2") |>     pip_add_from(pip1, "data") |>     pip_add_from(pip1, "data_prep") |>     pip_add_from(pip1, "standardize") |>     pip_add_from(pip2, "fit") |>     pip_add_from(pip2, "plot")  pip # <pipeflow_pip> cherry-picked-from-1-and-2 (5 steps) # --------------------------------------------------- #           step   depends    out state # 1:        data           [NULL]   new # 2:   data_prep      data [NULL]   new # 3: standardize data_prep [NULL]   new # 4:         fit      data [NULL]   new # 5:        plot  fit,data [NULL]   new

title: Collecting and filtering output
text: Tags let label steps meaningful keywords can later filter pipeline topic, stage, output type. Tags can set pipeline creation later using pip_set_tags(). used two families tags: "data"/"model" distinguish topic, "summary"/"plot"/"fit" output type. Whenever tags defined, shown pipeline overview: showing make use tags, let’s run pipeline inspect output individually previous vignettes.
code: library(pipeflow)  pip <- pip_new("my-pip") |>     pip_add(         "data",         function(data = airquality) data     ) |>     pip_add("data_prep",         function(data = ~data) {             replace(data, "Temp.Celsius", (data[, "Temp"] - 32) * 5 / 9)         },         tags = "data"                   # <-- set 'data' tag     ) |>     pip_add(         "data_summary",         function(             data = ~data_prep,             xVar = "Temp.Celsius",             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             format(summary(data[, c(xVar, yVar)])) |>                 as.data.frame(row.names = NA)         },         tags = c("data", "summary")     # <-- set 'data' and 'summary' tags     ) |>     pip_add(         "data_plot",         function(             data = ~data_prep,             xVar = "Temp.Celsius",             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             require(ggplot2, quietly = TRUE)             ggplot(data) +                 geom_point(aes(.data[[xVar]], .data[[yVar]])) +                 labs(title = "Data")         },         tags = c("data", "plot")        # <-- set 'data' and 'plot' tags     ) |>     pip_add(         "model_fit",         function(             data = ~data_prep,             xVar = "Temp.Celsius",             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             lm(paste(yVar, "~", xVar), data = data)         },         tags = c("model", "fit")        # <-- set 'model' and 'fit' tags     ) |>     pip_add(         "model_summary",         function(fit = ~model_fit) {             summary(fit) |>                 coefficients() |>                 as.data.frame()         },         tags = c("model", "summary")    # <-- set 'model' and 'summary' tags     ) |>     pip_add(         "model_plot",         function(             model = ~model_fit,             data_plot = ~data_plot,             xVar = "Temp.Celsius",             yVar = "Ozone"         ) {             coeffs <- coefficients(model)             data_plot +                 geom_abline(intercept = coeffs[1], slope = coeffs[2]) +                 labs(title = "Linear model fit")         },         tags = c("model", "plot")       # <-- set 'model' and 'plot' tags     ) pip # <pipeflow_pip> my-pip (7 steps) # ------------------------------- #             step             depends    out state          tags # 1:          data                     [NULL]   new               # 2:     data_prep                data [NULL]   new          data # 3:  data_summary           data_prep [NULL]   new  data,summary # 4:     data_plot           data_prep [NULL]   new     data,plot # 5:     model_fit           data_prep [NULL]   new     model,fit # title: pipeflow vs targets
text: {targets} widely used pipeline toolkit R ecosystem de-facto standard heavy-duty reproducible workflows. table contrasts two packages help decide one fits project. short, {targets} tool choice large-scale reproducible projects: persists results disk, captures provenance tar_meta(), scales distributed infrastructure via crew. {pipeflow} prioritises speed interactivity — sub-millisecond skipped-step checks, -session pipeline modification, low response times make well suited Shiny backend rapid parameter exploration analysis.

title: pipeflow vs targets
text: benchmarks provide quantitative comparison three pipeline topologies. timings measured system.time() across 30 iterations per scenario. Package versions: pipeflow 0.2.3.9005, targets 1.12.0. three scenarios chosen isolate different aspects pipeline overhead: Walkthrough — minimal 4-step pipeline mimicking typical exploratory workflow. measures startup cost skip-check overhead simplest realistic case. Long linear pipeline — chain N steps (s0 -> s1 -> ... -> sN). isolates per-step bookkeeping cost pipeline grows 128 steps. Fan-DAG — single source feeds N parallel branches converge one sink. tests package handles wide dependency structures. targets, iteration uses fresh tar_dir() tar_script() ensure fair timing end--end pipeline execution. pipeflow, pipelines built timed force = TRUE measure cost full re-run.

code: # Add steps with lambda functions p <- Pipeline$new("myPipe", data = 1) p$add("s1", \(x = ~data) 2*x)  # use input data p$add("s2", \(x = ~data, y = ~s1) x * y) try(p$add("s2", \(z = 3) 3)) # error: step 's2' exists already try(p$add("s3", \(z = ~foo) 3)) # dependency 'foo' not found p  # Add step with existing function p <- Pipeline$new("myPipe", data = c(1, 2, NA, 3, 4)) p$add("calc_mean", mean, params = list(x = ~data, na.rm = TRUE)) p$run()$get_out("calc_mean")  # Step description p <- Pipeline$new("myPipe", data = 1:10) p$add("s1", \(x = ~data) 2*x, description = "multiply by 2") print(p) print(p, verbose = TRUE) # print all columns  # Group output p <- Pipeline$new("myPipe", data = data.frame(x = 1:5, y = 1:5)) p$add("prep_x", \(data = ~data) data$x, group = "prep") p$add("prep_y", \(data = ~data) (data$y)^2, group = "prep") p$add("sum", \(x = ~prep_x, y = ~prep_y) x + y) p$run()$collect_out(all = TRUE)

title: Add pipeline step — pipe_add
text: pipeline consists series steps, usually added one one. step made function computing something pipeline run. function can existing R function (e.g. mean()) anonymous/lambda function specifically defined pipeline. One useful feature function parameters can refer results earlier pipeline steps using syntax x = ~earlier_step_name - see Examples details.

title: Add pipeline step — pipe_add
text: pip Pipeline object step string name step. step name must unique. fun function name function applied step. existing anonymous/lambda functions can used. function parameters must default values. parameter missing default value function signature, alternatively, can set via params argument (see Examples section mean() function). params list list parameters set overwrite parameters passed function. description string optional description step group string output collected pipeline execution (see pipe_collect_out() grouped defined group names. default, name step, comes handy pipeline copy-appended multiple times keep results function/step grouped one place. keepOut logical FALSE (default) output step collected calling pipe_collect_out() pipeline run. option used keep results matter skip intermediate results needed. See also function pipe_collect_out() details.

text: name name Pipeline data optional data used start pipeline. data also can set later using pipe_set_data() function. logger custom logger used logging. logger provided, default logger used, sufficient use cases. want use custom log function, need provide function obeys following form: function(level, msg, ...) { custom logging code } level argument string one info, warn, error. msg argument string containing message logged. ... argument list named parameters, can used add additional information log message. Currently, used add context case step giving warning error. Note default logger, log layout can altered time via set_log_layout().

text: pip Pipeline object step string name step. step name must unique. fun function name function applied step. existing anonymous/lambda functions can used. function parameters must default values. parameter missing default value function signature, alternatively, can set via params argument (see Examples section mean() function). params list list parameters set overwrite parameters passed function. description string optional description step group string output collected pipeline execution (see function pipe_collect_out()) grouped defined group names. default, name step, comes handy pipeline copy-appended multiple times keep results function/step grouped one place. keepOut logical FALSE (default) output step collected calling pipe_collect_out() pipeline run. option used keep results matter skip intermediate results needed. See also function pipe_collect_out() details.

title: Add a step — pip_add
text: Adds named step pipeline. step function whose parameters either hold constant defaults reference output prior step using formula notation (~step_name). Dependencies validated step added.

title: Add a step — pip_add
code: pip_add(x, step, fun, tags = character(0), after = length(x), exec = "auto")

title: Add a step — pip_add
text: x pipeflow pipeline object. step Unique step name. fun Function execute step. function parameter must default value. Default values simple constants resolved immediately. Default values formulas like ~other_step treated dependencies steps resolved respective output values runtime step executed. tags Optional character vector tags belonging step. Can also adjusted later using [pip_tag()]. Optional position new step inserted (defaults last position). Can step name integer index. set 0, new step inserted beginning pipeline. exec Execution mode step. One "auto", "split", "reduce" "plain". Using execution mode exec = split, output step marked partitioned output. mode, step depends split step (directly indirectly) output automatically mapped partition-wise step execution. reduce mode expects partitioned input passes without mapping, plain mode accepts non-partitioned input always intends execute single call. summary: auto: map partitioned input appears, otherwise single call split: single call, mark output partitioned reduce: single call, valid partitioned input plain: single call, valid non-partitioned input

title: Create a pipeline view — pip_view
code: p <- pip_new() pip_add(p, "load_raw", \(x = 1) x,   tags = c("io", "core", "daily") ) pip_add(p, "fit_model", \(x = 2) x + 1,   tags = c("model") ) pip_add(p, "eval_model", \(x = ~fit_model) x,   tags = c("model", "daily", "report") )  # Filter by a fixed column value (one or more states) pip_view(p, filter = list(state = "new")) #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (3 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>        step   depends    out state               tags #>    load_raw           [NULL]   new      io,core,daily #>   fit_model           [NULL]   new              model #>  eval_model fit_model [NULL]   new model,daily,report  # Combine filters: step pattern AND state pip_view(p, filter = list(step = "model", state = "new")) #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (0 of 3 steps) #> ----------------------------------------  # Filter by tag — keeps steps that have *any* of the given tags pip_view(p, tags = "daily") #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (2 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>        step   depends    out state               tags #>    load_raw           [NULL]   new      io,core,daily #>  eval_model fit_model [NULL]   new model,daily,report  # Combine explicit step selection with a filter (intersection) pip_view(p,   i      = c("load_raw", "fit_model"),   filter = list(state = "new") ) #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (2 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>       step depends    out state          tags #>   load_raw         [NULL]   new io,core,daily #>  fit_model         [NULL]   new         model  # Select by integer row indices pip_view(p, i = c(1L, 2L), filter = list(state = "new")) #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (2 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>       step depends    out state          tags #>   load_raw         [NULL]   new io,core,daily #>  fit_model         [NULL]   new         model  # Use a regex pattern to match step names pip_view(p, filter = list(step = "_model$"), fixed = FALSE) #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (2 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>        step   depends    out state               tags #>   fit_model           [NULL]   new              model #>  eval_model fit_model [NULL]   new model,daily,report  # Views are composable: create a view-of-view for progressive narrowing v1 <- pip_view(p, tags = "daily") print(v1) # load_raw, eval_model #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view (2 of 3 steps) #> ---------------------------------------- #>        step   depends    out state               tags #>    load_raw           [NULL]   new      io,core,daily #>  eval_model fit_model [NULL]   new model,daily,report v2 <- pip_view(v1, tags = "report") print(v2) # eval_model only #> <pipeflow_view> pipe view view (1 of 3 steps) #> --------------------------------------------- #>        step   depends    out state               tags #>  eval_model fit_model [NULL]   new model,daily,report
